blurb: A refined prairie native with silvery, ferny foliage and spikes of tiny purple flowers lit by orange anthers in early summer — a nitrogen-fixing, deep-rooted legume that thrives on neglect.
seasons: Silvery ferny foliage; purple-and-orange flower spikes early–midsummer; tan seed spikes after
wildlife: Native bees and butterflies; larval host for several butterflies and moths
deer: Browsed by deer and rabbits (palatable) — may need protection

care.hardiness: A winter-hardy Great Plains native (eastern Colorado included), comfortable through Boulder's zone 5b–6a with good drought and even some shade tolerance.[1]
care.planting: Plant in spring or fall into lean, well-drained soil and a sunny spot. Because it has a deep, branching woody taproot it resents transplanting — set out young plants and disturb the roots as little as possible; established plants are best left in place.[1]
care.sun: Full sun for best flowering and dense form, though it tolerates light shade.[1]
care.soil: Does best in sandy to silt-textured, well-drained soils and tolerates weakly acidic to moderately alkaline and even weakly saline ground — well suited to lean Front Range sites.[1]
care.water: A nitrogen-fixing prairie legume with good drought tolerance; give occasional water to establish, then little supplemental water in our semi-arid climate.[1]
care.spacing: A low subshrub to about 3.3 ft (1 m) tall; space roughly 2–3 ft apart in a planting or meadow.[1]
care.propagation: Seed has a hard coat and needs scarification plus stratification — e.g. soak in 180–200°F water for 12 hours then cold-moist chill at 41°F for ~30 days, or simply fall-sow to stratify over winter; inoculate with nitrogen-fixing bacteria. It can also be grown from greenwood (summer) or hardwood (fall) cuttings or suckers, but its deep taproot makes transplanting and division unreliable.[1]
care.bloom: Dense 2.4–4 in spikes of tiny purple flowers with bright orange anthers open in late June to July over silvery, lead-gray foliage — a magnet for native bees.[1]
care.maintenance: Slow-growing and low-maintenance; it tolerates mowing or a hard cutback (it resprouts from the woody base) but otherwise needs little. Avoid crowding by aggressive grasses while it establishes.[1]
care.feeding: No feeding needed — as a legume it fixes its own nitrogen and actually performs best on lean soil.[1]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — Leadplant (Amorpha canescens)

edible.summary: Grown as a native ornamental and pollinator plant, not a food; it has a documented traditional leaf tea but no edible food use.[1]
edible.caution: Leadplant isn't eaten as food. Many tribes brewed a medicinal 'tea' from its dried leaves and used it in various remedies, but that is a traditional infusion, not a safety-vetted food. No part is notably poisonous, though it shouldn't be eaten.[1]
